tier_2_handbook.definition: Bladder cancer is a malignant neoplasm arising from the urothelium, the specialized transitional epithelium lining the urinary bladder, renal pelvis, ureters, and proximal two-thirds of the urethra. Urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma is the most common histology in the United States and Europe [MS-7]. Approximately 75% of newly diagnosed cases present as non–muscle-invasive disease (Ta, T1, CIS), while the remainder are muscle-invasive (≥T2) or metastatic [MS-4]. The bladder is a hollow, muscular organ located in the pelvis, with the urothelium serving as the first line of defense against urine-borne toxins. Embryologically, the urothelium derives from the urogenital sinus and mesonephric duct contributions. The AJCC TNM staging system (8th edition) defines depth of invasion (T category), nodal involvement (N), and distant metastasis (M) [ST-1]. Clinical staging combines bimanual examination under anesthesia, endoscopic resection, and imaging, while pathologic staging requires cystectomy with lymph node dissection [BL-1]. The natural history ranges from indolent low-grade papillary tumors to aggressive high-grade carcinomas with metastatic potential.

subtypes.name: Conventional urothelial carcinoma (infiltrating)
subtypes.description: The most common type of invasive bladder cancer, composed of tumor cells with urothelial differentiation. Includes variants with squamous differentiation, glandular differentiation, or trophoblastic differentiation.
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 90% of bladder cancers in Western countries are urothelial; within this, conventional histology predominates.
subtypes.key_associations: Smoking, occupational exposures; FGFR3 mutations more common in low-grade tumors; TP53 mutations in high-grade tumors.

subtypes.name: Noninvasive papillary urothelial carcinoma (low-grade and high-grade)
subtypes.description: Papillary neoplasms confined to the mucosa (Ta). Low-grade have minimal cytologic atypia; high-grade show marked atypia and architectural disarray.
subtypes.frequency: Ta tumors represent 70–75% of NMIBC; low-grade Ta is the most common subtype in screening populations.
subtypes.key_associations: Recurrence common, progression less frequent; FGFR3 alterations frequent in low-grade; PUNLMP is a related entity with very low malignant potential.

subtypes.name: Urothelial carcinoma in situ (CIS)
subtypes.description: High-grade noninvasive flat lesion confined to the mucosa. Often multifocal and associated with invasive disease.
subtypes.frequency: 5–10% of newly diagnosed bladder cancers; commonly found in high-risk NMIBC.
subtypes.key_associations: High risk of progression to muscle-invasive disease (50% within 5 years if untreated); often p53 mutated.

subtypes.name: Micropapillary urothelial carcinoma
subtypes.description: Aggressive subtype characterized by small papillary clusters of tumor cells in lacunar spaces, mimicking serous carcinoma.
subtypes.key_associations: High risk of progression; even NMIBC often treated with early cystectomy; associated with poor prognosis [BL-D].

subtypes.name: Plasmacytoid urothelial carcinoma
subtypes.description: Subtype with discobesive cells with plasmacytoid morphology, often with signet-ring features.
subtypes.key_associations: High risk of peritoneal carcinomatosis; poor prognosis; CDH1 mutations common [BL-D].

subtypes.name: Sarcomatoid urothelial carcinoma
subtypes.description: Biphasic tumor with both carcinomatous and sarcomatoid (mesenchymal) components.
subtypes.key_associations: Aggressive behavior; high stage at presentation; poor response to conventional chemotherapy.

subtypes.name: Small cell / neuroendocrine carcinoma
subtypes.description: High-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma similar to small cell lung cancer. Can be pure or mixed with urothelial carcinoma.
subtypes.key_associations: Aggressive, early metastasis; brain imaging recommended; treated with small cell lung cancer regimens [BL-D].

subtypes.name: Squamous cell carcinoma (pure)
subtypes.description: Exclusively squamous differentiation with keratinization and/or intercellular bridges. Must exclude mixed histology.
subtypes.frequency: 3% of bladder tumors in the US; up to 75% in regions endemic for Schistosoma haematobium.
subtypes.key_associations: Chronic infection/inflammation (schistosomiasis, long-term catheterization); no proven role for perioperative chemotherapy; treated with surgery or chemoradiation [BL-D].

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma (including urachal carcinoma)
subtypes.description: Pure glandular neoplasm. Urachal carcinoma arises in the urachal remnant at bladder dome.
subtypes.key_associations: Urachal type: en bloc resection of urachal ligament with umbilicus; non-urachal: treat as colorectal cancer metastases; no proven role for neoadjuvant chemotherapy [BL-D].

subtypes.name: Primary bladder sarcoma
subtypes.description: Mesenchymal tumors including rhabdomyosarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, angiosarcoma, etc.
subtypes.key_associations: Treated per NCCN Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma [BL-D].

subtypes.name: Other rare subtypes
subtypes.description: Includes nested, tubular/microcystic, lymphoepithelioma-like, giant cell, lipid-rich, clear cell, poorly differentiated, and others.
subtypes.key_associations: Some (e.g., lymphoepithelioma-like) may have better prognosis; most treated as conventional urothelial carcinoma.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Bladder cancer is characterized by a high mutational burden, ranking third among all cancers per The Cancer Genome Atlas [251,252]. Comprehensive genomic profiling of advanced urothelial carcinoma revealed a mean of 2.6 clinically relevant genetic alterations per case, with the most frequent being CDKN2A (34%), FGFR3 (21%), PIK3CA (20%), and ERBB2 (17%) [253]. FGFR3 alterations (including sequence variants, copy number variants, and rearrangements) are particularly common in low-grade Ta tumors and define a subset responsive to erdafitinib therapy [291,295]. TP53 mutations are hallmark of high-grade, muscle-invasive disease and are associated with genomic instability. The panel recommends evaluation of microsatellite instability (MSI) and mismatch repair (MMR) status for metastatic disease, as MSI-H tumors may benefit from immunotherapy [BL-I 3 of 3]. HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+ or 2+) is found in approximately 20% of advanced bladder cancers, enabling treatment with fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki [34,303]. Molecular classification into luminal, basal, and other subtypes is being explored but not yet routine. Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) has emerged as a prognostic and predictive biomarker after cystectomy, with FDA-approved tumor-informed multiplex PCR-NGS assays recommended for risk stratification and to determine use of adjuvant immunotherapy [BL-I 2 of 3]. In NMIBC, biomarkers are currently limited to research settings [BL-I 2 of 3]. Germline testing is recommended for patients with younger age or Lynch syndrome-related personal/family history, as Lynch syndrome (especially MSH2 mutations) strongly predisposes to upper tract and bladder urothelial carcinoma [7,11].
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: The American Cancer Society estimates 84,530 new cases of urinary bladder cancer (64,730 males and 19,800 females) and 17,870 deaths (12,640 males and 5,230 females) in the United States in 2026 [1]. Bladder cancer is the sixth most common cancer in the US, with a median age at diagnosis of 73 years; it is rarely diagnosed in individuals under age 40 [2]. Age-adjusted incidence is approximately 3–4 times higher in males than females, and White individuals have a significantly higher incidence than Black individuals [1,3]. Globally, bladder cancer incidence and mortality vary widely, with the highest rates in Southern and Western Europe, Northern America, and Northern Africa, partly due to Schistosoma haematobium infection in endemic areas [5]. In the US, mortality rates have been declining over the past few decades, but racial disparities persist, with Black patients having lower incidence but higher mortality [5]. The clinical spectrum is divided into non–muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC; ~75% of new cases), muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC; ~20–25%), and metastatic disease (~5% at initial presentation) [2,20]. Five-year relative survival ranges from 96% for NMIBC (in situ) to 69% for localized disease, 38% for regional disease, and 6% for distant metastatic disease [2].

risk_factors.detail: Strongest known risk factor; accounts for approximately 50% of cases in men and 30% in women. Former smokers remain at elevated risk, though the risk decreases with cessation [10].
risk_factors.strength: Strong (RR 3-4 for current smokers; dose-response relationship)

risk_factors.detail: Aromatic amines (e.g., benzidine, beta-naphthylamine) used in dye, rubber, leather, and printing industries; also polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [6].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate to strong (attributable fraction ~18% in some studies)

risk_factors.detail: Prior radiation for prostate, cervical, or other pelvic cancers increases risk of bladder cancer, especially high-grade tumors [3].
Cyclophosphamide (alkylating agent) increases risk; pioglitazone use for diabetes has been associated with increased risk [3].
Chronic urinary tract infections, long-term indwelling catheters, and Schistosoma haematobium infection (especially for squamous cell carcinoma) [3,5].
risk_factors.strength: Strong for Schistosoma in endemic areas; moderate for catheter-related

risk_factors.detail: Incidence is 3–4 times higher in males than females, though females present with more advanced stage [1].
Incidence is approximately 2 times higher in White individuals than in Black individuals in the US, though Black individuals have higher mortality [1,3].
Personal or family history of bladder cancer increases risk; Lynch syndrome (especially MSH2 mutations) strongly predisposes to urothelial carcinoma [7,11].
Obesity (BMI >30) and type 2 diabetes are associated with modestly increased risk; diabetes may act through insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia [4].
High fluid intake may be protective; low intake may concentrate carcinogens in urine; not quantified in the source as a primary risk factor but suggested.
risk_factors.strength: Insufficient evidence; not listed as major risk factor in source

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Bladder cancer most commonly presents with microscopic or gross hematuria, which is the classic alarm symptom. Approximately 75% of newly detected cases are non–muscle-invasive disease (Ta, T1, CIS) [20]. Other frequent presentations include urinary frequency, urgency, or dysuria due to bladder irritation or reduced capacity. Less commonly, patients may present with a urinary tract infection or, in advanced disease, upper tract obstruction or flank pain. The median age at diagnosis is 73 years, and the disease is rare in individuals <40 years [1,2]. Risk factors include smoking, male sex, white race, occupational exposures, and Lynch syndrome [3-7]. A substantial hereditary component is recognized, particularly with Lynch syndrome [7,11]. Initial evaluation includes office cystoscopy, urine cytology, and imaging of the upper tract before TURBT [BL-1].

symptoms.name: Hematuria
symptoms.detail: Microscopic or gross hematuria; most common presenting symptom.
symptoms.frequency: Most common (present in majority of patients).
symptoms.alarm: Yes – requires urgent cystoscopy and imaging.

symptoms.name: Urinary frequency/urgency
symptoms.detail: Irritative voiding symptoms secondary to tumor-associated inflammation or reduced bladder capacity.

symptoms.name: Urinary tract infection
symptoms.detail: May be the presenting complaint, especially if irritative symptoms are present.
symptoms.alarm: Low – but should prompt urine culture and cystoscopy if recurrent.

symptoms.name: Upper tract obstruction/pain
symptoms.detail: Flank pain or hydronephrosis from ureteral involvement in advanced disease.

signs.name: Cystoscopic lesion
signs.detail: Visible papillary or sessile tumor on office cystoscopy.
signs.frequency: Nearly all patients with bladder cancer have a lesion visible on cystoscopy.

signs.name: Palpable mass on EUA
signs.detail: Bimanual examination under anesthesia may reveal a palpable mass if muscle-invasive or extravesical disease.
signs.frequency: More common in advanced stages; frequency not specified.

signs.name: Positive urine cytology
signs.detail: Exfoliated malignant urothelial cells in urine.
signs.frequency: Variable; sensitivity higher for high-grade tumors and CIS.

atypical_or_missed: Positive urine cytology with negative cystoscopy and imaging, requiring evaluation of upper tract and prostatic urethra (BL-5).
Irritative voiding symptoms alone may be misattributed to urinary tract infection, delaying diagnosis.
Incidental detection during imaging for other indications.
Bladder cancer may present with only microhematuria, which can be overlooked.
Presence of variant histology (e.g., micropapillary, plasmacytoid) may alter natural history and require aggressive management.

red_flags: Gross hematuria (visible blood in urine) – requires immediate urologic evaluation.
Persistent microscopic hematuria in patients with risk factors (age >40, smoking, occupational exposures).
Urinary frequency, urgency, or dysuria without infection or refractory to treatment.
Flank pain or hydronephrosis suggesting ureteral obstruction.
Palpable mass on bimanual examination under anesthesia.
Positive urine cytology even with normal cystoscopy – may indicate CIS or upper tract tumor.
Family history of Lynch syndrome or personal history of Lynch-related cancers.
Age <40 with hematuria – consider germline testing.
Sessile or high-grade tumor on cystoscopy – high risk of muscle invasion.
Rapid recurrence after initial TURBT or intravesical therapy.

diagnostic.rationale: Direct visualization of bladder lesions; can be enhanced with blue light or narrow-band imaging.
diagnostic.key_findings: Papillary or sessile tumor; flat erythematous patches for CIS; visible lesion in most cases.

diagnostic.rationale: Histologic diagnosis and initial staging; must include muscularis propria for accurate staging.
diagnostic.key_findings: Depth of invasion (Ta, T1, T2+), histologic grade, presence of CIS, lymphovascular invasion.

diagnostic.rationale: Detection of malignant urothelial cells; high specificity.
diagnostic.key_findings: Positive in high-grade tumors and CIS; may be negative in low-grade Ta.

diagnostic.rationale: Improved detection of flat lesions (CIS) and small tumors.
diagnostic.key_findings: Increased detection of CIS compared to white light cystoscopy.

diagnostic.rationale: Bimanual assessment of tumor fixity and local extent.
diagnostic.key_findings: Palpable mass suggests muscle-invasive or extravesical disease.

staging.rationale: Preferred imaging for upper tract evaluation and local staging.
staging.indication: All patients before TURBT to assess collecting system and local extent; also used for follow-up.

staging.rationale: Alternative when CTU is contraindicated (poor renal function, contrast allergy).
staging.indication: Patients with contraindications to iodinated contrast; may also be used for local staging with pelvic MRI.
For muscle-invasive or metastatic disease (MIBC); chest CT preferred over x-ray for sensitivity.

staging.test: Bone scan or MRI of spine/pelvis
staging.indication: Clinical suspicion (elevated alkaline phosphatase, bone pain) or symptoms; not routine for NMIBC.

staging.rationale: May alter management in high-risk MIBC.
staging.indication: Selected patients with ≥cT2 disease, especially ≥cT3; not for upper tract delineation.

staging.test: Abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI with IV contrast
staging.indication: For MIBC; may be performed with CTU; MRI pelvis without and with contrast for local staging.

staging.rationale: Baseline assessment; alkaline phosphatase may be elevated with bone metastases.

staging.indication: Before neoadjuvant or first-line chemotherapy; consider timed urine collection for borderline results.

biomarkers.test: FGFR3 genomic alterations (including sequence variants, copy number variants, rearrangements/fusions)
biomarkers.rationale: Identify patients eligible for erdafitinib (FGFR inhibitor).
biomarkers.when: At initial diagnosis of unresectable or metastatic bladder cancer (stage IV); also at recurrence.

biomarkers.rationale: Identify patients eligible for fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki.
biomarkers.when: At initial diagnosis of unresectable or metastatic bladder cancer.

biomarkers.test: Microsatellite instability (MSI) and/or mismatch repair (MMR) status
biomarkers.rationale: Identify patients eligible for checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
biomarkers.when: For all patients with unresectable or metastatic bladder cancer.

biomarkers.test: Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) for minimal residual disease (MRD)
biomarkers.rationale: Prognostic and predictive for adjuvant immunotherapy after cystectomy.
biomarkers.when: In patients with MIBC post-cystectomy who have not received prior immune checkpoint inhibitor; using FDA-approved, tumor-informed multiplex PCR-NGS assay.

biomarkers.rationale: Identify Lynch syndrome and other hereditary cancer syndromes.
biomarkers.when: Consider at initial diagnosis if younger age (<40) or personal/family history of Lynch-related cancers.

do_not_miss: Muscle (muscularis propria) in the TURBT specimen for accurate staging.
Repeat TURBT within 6 weeks for high-grade Ta/T1, incomplete resection, or no muscle in specimen.
Immediate intravesical chemotherapy (gemcitabine preferred, or mitomycin) within 24 hours of TURBT if non-muscle-invasive suspected and no bladder perforation.
Upper tract imaging (CTU or MRU) before TURBT.
GFR assessment to determine cisplatin eligibility before perioperative or first-line therapy.
Molecular testing for FGFR3, HER2, and MSI/MMR in advanced/metastatic disease.
Enrollment in clinical trials when available, especially for BCG-unresponsive NMIBC and metastatic disease.
avoid: Routine mapping biopsies of normal-appearing urothelium – low yield, can cause additional damage [15-18].
Chest imaging for initial staging of NMIBC – not recommended [BL-A 1 of 5].
Immediate intravesical chemotherapy if bladder perforation is suspected.
Substituting carboplatin for cisplatin in the perioperative setting – no survival benefit [BL-G 2 of 8].
Routine bone imaging in NMIBC – bone metastasis is unlikely [BL-A 1 of 5].
Routine brain imaging in bladder cancer except for small cell histology or symptoms [BL-A 3 of 5].
Routine use of FDG-PET/CT for NMIBC staging.
Carboplatin as a radiation sensitizer – not effective [BL-G 6 of 8].

t_stages.description: Tumor invades lamina propria (subepithelial connective tissue).
Tumor invades superficial muscularis propria (inner half).
Tumor invades deep muscularis propria (outer half).
Tumor invades perivesical tissue macroscopically (extravesical mass).
Extravesical tumor invades prostatic stroma, seminal vesicles, uterus, vagina.
Extravesical tumor invades pelvic wall, abdominal wall.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
Single regional lymph node metastasis in the true pelvis (perivesical, obturator, internal and external iliac, or sacral lymph node).
Multiple regional lymph node metastases in the true pelvis (perivesical, obturator, internal and external iliac, or sacral lymph nodes).
Lymph node metastasis to the common iliac lymph nodes.

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis limited to lymph nodes beyond the common iliacs.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Noninvasive papillary carcinoma, no nodal or distant spread.
Flat carcinoma in situ, high risk of progression if untreated.
Tumor invades lamina propria, no nodal or distant spread.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – TURBT ± intravesical therapy; repeat TURBT for high-risk.
Curative – neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy + cystectomy or bladder preservation with chemoradiation.

stage_groups.criteria: T3a, N0, M0; T3b, N0, M0; T4a, N0, M0; T1–T4a, N1, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Extravesical tumor extension or single regional lymph node metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative – neoadjuvant chemotherapy + cystectomy or chemoradiation; in N1, may need systemic therapy first.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Multiple regional lymph node metastases or common iliac node involvement.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative – induction systemic therapy or chemoradiation; subsequent consolidation if response.

stage_groups.criteria: T4b, Any N, M0; Any T, Any N, M1a
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invades pelvic/abdominal wall or distant nodes beyond common iliacs.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative or potentially curative in select responders – systemic therapy ± consolidative local therapy.
Palliative – systemic therapy; metastasectomy in highly selected oligometastatic patients.

staging_pearls: Clinical staging (c) uses bimanual EUA, TURBT, and imaging; pathologic staging (p) uses cystectomy and lymph node dissection [BL-1].
Depth of invasion is the most important determinant of prognosis and treatment for localized bladder cancer.
Muscularis propria must be present in the TURBT specimen for accurate staging; absence may lead to understaging (49% understaged without muscle vs 14% with muscle) [119,120].
pT1 substaging (microscopic/focal vs extensive invasion) may have prognostic value but is not yet widely standardized [21-23].
The 8th AJCC edition subdivides stage III into IIIA and IIIB, and stage IV into IVA and IVB [19].
Positive urine cytology with negative cystoscopy requires evaluation of upper tracts and prostatic urethra to identify occult disease [BL-5].
Understaging is common: 42% of patients are upstaged after cystectomy [155].
Histologic grade is reported as low-grade or high-grade for urothelial carcinomas; for squamous/adenocarcinoma, G1–G3 is used [ST-1].

prognosis.overall: Bladder cancer is the sixth most common cancer in the United States, with an estimated 84,530 new cases and 17,870 deaths expected in 2026 [1]. The median age at diagnosis is 73 years [2]. The clinical spectrum is divided into non–muscle-invasive (NMIBC), muscle-invasive (MIBC), and metastatic disease, each with distinct prognosis and management. For NMIBC, 31% to 78% of patients experience recurrence within 5 years depending on initial stage, grade, size, and multiplicity [24]. Untreated carcinoma in situ (CIS) progresses to muscle-invasive disease in 50% of patients within 5 years; even with treatment, 30% to 40% progress within 10 years [125]. For MIBC, neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radical cystectomy improves median survival from 46 to 77 months compared to cystectomy alone [163]. Bladder-preserving trimodality therapy yields 5-year overall survival (OS) rates of approximately 49% to 73% in selected patients [220,227,228]. In the metastatic setting, first-line enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab improved median OS to 31.5 months versus 16.1 months with platinum-based chemotherapy (HR 0.47; 95% CI 0.38–0.58) [260]. Avelumab maintenance therapy after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy provides a median OS of 21.4 months versus 14.3 months with best supportive care (HR 0.69; 95% CI 0.56–0.86) [282].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated; recurrence rate 31-78% within 5 years [24]
by_stage.context: Low-risk includes solitary Ta low-grade ≤3 cm; TURBT alone is often curative.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: 50% progress to MIBC within 5 years if untreated; with treatment, 30-40% progress within 10 years [125]
by_stage.context: High-risk includes T1 high-grade, CIS, multifocal, large tumors; BCG induction + maintenance is recommended.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy + cystectomy: median OS 77 months vs 46 months with cystectomy alone [163]
by_stage.context: 5-year OS not provided; median survival reported.
Candidates: solitary tumors, no hydronephrosis, no extensive CIS.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not stated; median OS 31.5 months with EV+Pembro, 16.1 months with chemo [260]
by_stage.context: Enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab is preferred first-line category 1.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Median OS 21.4 months with avelumab maintenance vs 14.3 months BSC [282]
by_stage.context: Avelumab maintenance for patients without progression after platinum-based chemo.

prognostic_factors: NMIBC: tumor grade, stage (Ta vs T1), size, multiplicity, recurrence rate, presence of CIS, lymphovascular invasion, prostatic urethral invasion, histologic subtypes (micropapillary, plasmacytoid, sarcomatoid, small cell) [113,126]
MIBC: depth of invasion (T2 vs T3 vs T4), nodal status, lymphovascular invasion, margin status, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, histologic subtypes, molecular biomarkers (ctDNA, FGFR3, HER2)
Metastatic: performance status, presence of visceral metastases, alkaline phosphatase levels, prior response to platinum therapy
Biomarkers: ctDNA-MRD positivity post-cystectomy is prognostic; FGFR3 alterations predict response to erdafitinib; HER2 IHC 3+ or 2+ predicts response to fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan
prognosis.prognostic_context: Risk stratification for NMIBC follows AUA/SUO guidelines (low, intermediate, high) with further subclassification by the International Bladder Cancer Group [115,126]. The AJCC 8th edition staging system subgroups stage III and IV into IIIA/IIIB and IVA/IVB [19]. Molecular testing is recommended for advanced disease to identify actionable alterations (FGFR3, HER2, MSI/MMR). ctDNA-MRD testing is emerging as a tool for risk stratification and adjuvant immunotherapy decision-making after cystectomy [43 (Powles 2025)].

disease_related.management: Evaluation with cystoscopy, imaging; TURBT for visible lesions.
Stenting, percutaneous nephrostomy, systemic therapy, palliative RT.

disease_related.complication: Metastatic spread (lymph nodes, bone, liver, lung, CNS)
disease_related.management: Systemic therapy, palliative RT, metastasectomy in select cases.

chemotherapy:
Cisplatin-based: nephrotoxicity, neuropathy, ototoxicity, myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting, fatigue
- Growth factor support recommended for ddMVAC [171,197]
- Split-dose cisplatin (35 mg/m2 days 1 and 2 or days 1 and 8) for borderline renal function (category 2B) [BL-G 2 of 8]

Carboplatin-based: less renal toxicity but inferior efficacy in perioperative setting; myelosuppression common
- Gemcitabine/carboplatin used for cisplatin-ineligible metastatic patients [257]
ddMVAC: gastrointestinal disorders, asthenia, neutropenic fever [183,184]
GC: comparable toxicity to ddMVAC but different profile [184]
immunotherapy:
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, atezolizumab, avelumab, durvalumab): immune-related adverse events (irAEs) including pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, arthralgia, hyponatremia, fatigue, rash
- Grade ≥3 AEs reported in 13-18% of patients [109,286]
- Management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [a]
N-803 (nogapendekin alfa inbakicept-pmln): flu-like symptoms, urinary urgency; grade 1-2 AEs in 86% [107]
Pembrolizumab for NMIBC: arthralgia, hyponatremia; grade ≥3 AEs 13% [109]
targeted_therapy: Erdafitinib: hyperphosphatemia, stomatitis, diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome; grade ≥3 AEs 46% [291]; treatment-related deaths 0.7% [293]
Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan: interstitial lung disease (ILD) (10.5% adjudicated, 1.1% fatal), nausea, fatigue; grade ≥3 AEs 40.8% [303]
Enfortumab vedotin: peripheral sensory neuropathy, fatigue, alopecia, rash, neutropenia; grade ≥3 AEs 54-64% [258,296]; treatment-related deaths reported
surgery: TURBT: bladder perforation, bleeding, infection; immediate intravesical chemotherapy contraindicated if perforation suspected [BL-1]

Radical cystectomy: urinary diversion complications (ileal conduit: stoma issues, UTI, stones; neobladder: incontinence, retention), impotence, vaginal dryness, bowel dysfunction, thromboembolism, incisional hernia; mortality ~2% in high-volume centers
Partial cystectomy: similar complications, plus risk of local recurrence; requires adequate margins and no CIS
Pelvic lymphadenectomy: lymphocele, bleeding, nerve injury, thromboembolism
radiation:
External beam RT: radiation cystitis, proctitis, diarrhea, fatigue, skin reactions; late effects include bladder contracture, hematuria, bowel obstruction
- Grade ≥3 late genitourinary toxicity 5.7%, gastrointestinal 1.9% [230]
- Hypofractionated schedule (55 Gy/20 fractions) noninferior to standard [232]
Concurrent chemoradiotherapy: increased acute toxicity but manageable; no significant increase in late toxicity [231]
Adjuvant RT post-cystectomy: small risk of gastrointestinal toxicity [204]

supportive_care.overview: The NCCN Guidelines emphasize a multidisciplinary approach to supportive care, including screening for distress, social determinants of health, and smoking cessation [b, NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management]. Referral to the NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship, Palliative Care, and Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities is recommended. No single supportive care plan fits all patients; individualization based on disease stage, treatment modality, and patient preference is essential.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Not specifically detailed; NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship and Palliative Care include general recommendations for cancer-related malnutrition and cachexia. For patients undergoing cystectomy or chemoradiation, nutritional assessment and support (dietary counseling, oral supplements, or enteral/parenteral nutrition) may be indicated.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not explicitly provided; antiemetic prophylaxis should follow standard guidelines for emetogenic chemotherapy (e.g., cisplatin-based regimens require high-dose corticosteroids + NK1 antagonist + 5-HT3 antagonist). The guideline references growth factor support for ddMVAC but not specific antiemetics.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Growth factor support (G-CSF) is recommended for patients receiving ddMVAC (dose-dense MVAC) in both neoadjuvant and metastatic settings [171,197]. It is also recommended for other myelosuppressive regimens as clinically indicated, but no specific dosing schedule is provided.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not addressed in the bladder cancer-specific algorithm; general oncology VTE prophylaxis per NCCN Guidelines for Cancer-Associated Venous Thromboembolic Disease should be followed, particularly in patients undergoing major surgery or receiving chemotherapy.
supportive_care.pain_management: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care. Pain management for bone metastases includes palliative RT (30 Gy/10 fractions or 21 Gy/3 fractions) [BL-H 1 of 3]. For pelvic recurrence, consider palliative TURBT or RT.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Screening for distress using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List is recommended at initial evaluation [b]. Referral to psychosocial services, genetic counseling (if indicated), and patient advocacy resources (e.g., Bladder Cancer Advocacy Network) may be beneficial. The NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management provide further guidance.
supportive_care.dental_care: Not specifically mentioned. Patients receiving chemotherapy (especially cisplatin) should have dental evaluation and management of oral mucositis as per standard oncology supportive care guidelines. DPYD testing is recommended before fluoropyrimidine therapy [BL-G 6 of 8].

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Follow-up schedules are stratified by disease type and treatment modality. No single plan fits all; duration and frequency should be individualized and may extend beyond 5 years after shared decision-making [BL-A 1 of 5].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Early detection of recurrence (local, regional, or distant) and late effects of treatment. Recurrence patterns differ by initial stage and treatment. For preserved bladder, cystoscopy and urine cytology are key; for post-cystectomy, imaging of upper tracts and chest is essential. Vitamin B12 monitoring is needed after cystectomy with urinary diversion.
late_effects_screening: Vitamin B12 deficiency: annual B12 assessment after cystectomy with urinary diversion (especially continent diversion) [BL-E 3,4 of 6]
Renal function: electrolyte and creatinine monitoring every 3-6 months for first 2 years, then annually
Liver function: AST, ALT, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase every 3-6 months for first 2 years, then annually
Second primary urothelial tumors: upper tract imaging (CTU or MRU) at baseline and periodically for high-risk NMIBC (every 1-2 years up to 10 years) [BL-A 1 of 5]
Osteoporosis/ bone health: not specific; bone scan if symptoms
Cardiovascular: not specific; general cancer survivorship guidelines apply
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: NMIBC: most recurrences are non–muscle-invasive and can be treated endoscopically; progression to MIBC occurs in a subset. MIBC post-cystectomy: local recurrence 10-30%, distant metastases more common; upper tract recurrence 0.75-6.4% [238]. Post-bladder preservation: local recurrence in bladder or elsewhere in urothelial tract, plus distant metastases. Metastatic disease: progression on therapy is common; regular imaging every 3-6 months.

clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: Cystoscopy at 3 and 12 months, then annually up to 5 years; after 5 years as clinically indicated [BL-E 1 of 6]
Cystoscopy at 3, 6, 12 months; then every 6 months through year 5, then annually as clinically indicated. Urine cytology at 3, 6, 12 months, then every 6 months, then annually [BL-E 2 of 6]
Cystoscopy every 3 months for year 1, every 6 months for years 2-5, then annually. Urine cytology every 3 months for year 1, every 6 months for years 2-5, then annually [BL-E 2 of 6]

CTU/MRU at 3 and 12 months, then annually for up to 5 years; then renal US annually. Chest CT every 3-6 months for MIBC for first 2 years, then annually. Consider urine cytology/urethral wash every 6-12 months [BL-E 3,4 of 6]
Cystoscopy every 3 months for year 1, every 6 months for years 2-5, then annually. Imaging: CTU/MRU and chest CT every 3-6 months for MIBC for first 2 years, then annually [BL-E 5 of 6]
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 3-6 months if clinically indicated or with new symptoms. CBC/CMP every 1-3 months. B12 annually post-cystectomy [BL-E 6 of 6]

imaging_strategy: NMIBC: Upper tract imaging (CTU or MRU) at baseline for all; for high-risk NMIBC, repeat at 12 months and every 1-2 years up to 10 years [BL-A 1 of 5]
MIBC: CTU/MRU (or MRU if contrast contraindicated) for staging and follow-up. Chest CT preferred over x-ray. FDG-PET/CT (category 2B) may be used for high-risk or suspected metastasis [BL-A 2,3 of 5]
Post-cystectomy: CTU/MRU at 3 and 12 months, then annually; chest CT every 3-6 months for first 2 years [BL-E 3,4 of 6]
Metastatic: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 3-6 months; FDG-PET/CT may be used [BL-E 6 of 6]
Bone imaging: only for clinical suspicion or symptoms (MRI, FDG-PET/CT, or bone scan) [BL-A 3 of 5]
Brain MRI: only for symptomatic or high-risk patients (e.g., small cell histology) [BL-A 3 of 5]
laboratory_monitoring: Renal function (electrolytes, creatinine) every 3-6 months for first 2 years, then annually (post-cystectomy or on active therapy)
Liver function (AST, ALT, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase) every 3-6 months for first 2 years, then annually
CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) every 3-6 months if received chemotherapy
Vitamin B12 annually after cystectomy (especially with continent diversion) [BL-E 3,4,6 of 6]

Urine cytology: for intermediate/high-risk NMIBC at scheduled intervals; for post-cystectomy, consider every 6-12 months including urethral wash if high-risk features (positive urethral margin, multifocal CIS, prostatic urethral invasion) [BL-E 2,3,4 of 6]
supportive_follow_up: Smoking cessation counseling and treatment (NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation) [BL-1]
Distress screening using NCCN Distress Thermometer [b]
Genetic counseling referral if younger age or Lynch syndrome family history [BL-1]
Survivorship care per NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship (e.g., late effects, health promotion) [BL-E 1-6 of 6]
Palliative care referral for advanced disease (NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care)

special_situations.key_message: Prioritize BCG for high-risk NMIBC induction; use alternative intravesical agents or split-dose BCG as needed.
special_situations.management: Sequential gemcitabine/docetaxel, mitomycin, gemcitabine, epirubicin, valrubicin, docetaxel, or gemcitabine/mitomycin. Split-dose (1/3 or 1/2) BCG may be used. Maintenance BCG may be stopped at 1 year for high-risk. Induction prioritized over maintenance [BL-F 2 of 4].
special_situations.evidence: Multiple randomized trials and guidelines from AUA, SUO, BCAN [88,89]

special_situations.key_message: Preferred options are cystectomy or clinical trial. Other options include intravesical agents, checkpoint inhibitors, and novel therapies.
special_situations.management: For CIS±Ta/T1: cystectomy (preferred), clinical trial, BCG + N-803, gemcitabine intravesical system (TAR-200), nadofaragene firadenovec, multi-agent intravesical chemo, pembrolizumab (useful in certain circumstances). For papillary-only: similar plus single-agent intravesical chemo (category 2B). Non-cystectomy candidates: concurrent chemoradiotherapy for cT1 (category 2A) or cTa (category 2B) [BL-4].
special_situations.evidence: KEYNOTE-057 [109,111], QUILT-3.032 [107], SunRISe-1 [103], phase III nadofaragene [104]

special_situations.situation: Non-Urothelial Histologies (Small Cell, Adenocarcinoma, Squamous, Sarcoma)
special_situations.key_message: Treat based on histology-specific guidelines; perioperative chemotherapy not proven for squamous or adenocarcinoma.
special_situations.management: Small cell: treat per SCLC guidelines (neoadjuvant chemo + local therapy or chemoradiation). Alternating doxorubicin/ifosfamide with cisplatin/etoposide is an option [4-6]. Adenocarcinoma/urachal: surgery (partial/completive cystectomy with en bloc urachal ligament); consider colon cancer regimens for advanced. Squamous: surgery or chemoradiation; no proven role for neoadjuvant chemo. Sarcoma: treat per Soft Tissue Sarcoma guidelines [BL-D 1,2 of 2].
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective series and expert consensus [1-9 in BL-D]

special_situations.situation: Upper Tract Urothelial Carcinoma (UTUC) – Renal Pelvis and Ureter
special_situations.key_message: Management based on risk group (low, intermediate, high); nephroureterectomy with bladder cuff and perioperative intravesical chemo is standard for high-risk; neoadjuvant chemo may be considered.
special_situations.management: Low risk: endoscopic ablation or chemoablation + surveillance. Intermediate risk: endoscopic + intrapelvic therapy or radical nephroureterectomy (RNU). High risk: RNU + perioperative intravesical chemo + neoadjuvant chemo in select patients. Adjuvant: platinum-based chemo if pT3-4 or N+ and no neoadjuvant; nivolumab or pembrolizumab (category 2B) [UTT-2, UTT-4].

special_situations.key_message: Treatment depends on depth of invasion (mucosal vs ductal/acini vs stromal).
special_situations.management: Mucosal: TURP + BCG; ductal/acini: TURP + BCG or cystoprostatectomy; stromal: cystoprostatectomy + neoadjuvant chemo. Local recurrence: cystoprostatectomy [UCP-1].
special_situations.evidence: Systematic review and meta-analysis of intravesical BCG [349]

special_situations.situation: Primary Carcinoma of the Urethra (Male and Female)
special_situations.key_message: Treatment based on histology, location, and stage; rare disease, referral to specialized center recommended.
special_situations.management: CIS/Ta/T1: TUR + intraurethral therapy. T2: distal urethrectomy/partial penectomy (male) or chemoradiation vs urethrectomy+cystectomy (female). T3-4: chemoradiation (preferred) or neoadjuvant chemo+surgery. Node-positive: chemoradiation or systemic therapy+consolidative surgery. Advanced: systemic therapy per histology [PCU-2, PCU-3].

special_situations.key_message: Selected patients with favorable response to systemic therapy may benefit from metastasectomy.
special_situations.management: Consider metastasectomy after response to systemic therapy, especially for solitary lung or lymph node metastases. No randomized data; retrospective studies show 5-year OS 28-72% after metastasectomy [247]. Should be performed in experienced centers.
special_situations.evidence: Phase II trial [240], retrospective series [242-245], meta-analysis [247]

special_situations.key_message: ctDNA-MRD positivity within 1 year post-cystectomy can guide adjuvant atezolizumab (category 1) in patients without prior immunotherapy.
special_situations.management: For ctDNA-MRD positive patients (by FDA-approved personalized tumor-informed assay), atezolizumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy (category 1) regardless of prior neoadjuvant therapy. This applies to both MIBC and UTUC in the context of the IMvigor010 ctDNA analysis [43].

special_situations.key_message: Consider germline multigene panel testing and genetic counselor referral for younger patients or those with personal/family history of Lynch syndrome-related cancers.
special_situations.management: Initial evaluation includes family history. Criteria from NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric [BL-1, c]. Lynch syndrome is associated with UTUC and bladder cancer [7,11].
special_situations.evidence: Carlo et al. 2020 [7], van der Post et al. 2010 [11]

clinical_pearls: Immediate post-TURBT intravesical gemcitabine (category 1, preferred) or mitomycin (category 1) reduces 5-year recurrence by ~35% in selected patients with low-grade, low-volume Ta disease; not effective in patients with EORTC recurrence score ≥5 or ≥8 tumors [12-14].

For BCG-naïve high-risk NMIBC, BCG induction + maintenance is category 1 for patients without very-high-risk features (lymphovascular invasion, prostatic urethral involvement, variant histology) [130]. For BCG-unresponsive NMIBC, cystectomy or clinical trial is preferred; pembrolizumab is reserved for those who cannot undergo cystectomy [BL-4].

Neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy (ddMVAC preferred over GC based on VESPER trial) is category 1 for stage II and IIIA MIBC prior to radical cystectomy [183-185]. Carboplatin should not be substituted in the perioperative setting [BL-G 2 of 8].
For cisplatin-ineligible patients with MIBC, perioperative enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab followed by cystectomy is an option based on KEYNOTE-905 [6].

First-line treatment for advanced/metastatic urothelial carcinoma: enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab is the preferred category 1 regimen regardless of cisplatin eligibility, based on EV-302 (median OS 31.5 vs 16.1 months, HR 0.47) [260]. Avelumab maintenance is category 1 after first-line platinum-based chemo without progression [282].
In the second-line setting (post-platinum and post-checkpoint inhibitor), enfortumab vedotin (category 1) or erdafitinib (category 1 for FGFR3-altered) are preferred options [293,297].

Bladder preservation with trimodality therapy (maximal TURBT + concurrent chemoradiotherapy) is category 1 for stage II/IIIA MIBC in appropriate candidates (solitary tumor, no hydronephrosis, no extensive CIS, good bladder function) [BL-6, BL-H 1 of 3].

ctDNA-MRD testing using an FDA-approved personalized tumor-informed assay is recommended post-cystectomy for risk stratification and to guide adjuvant atezolizumab (category 1) in patients without prior immunotherapy [43] (BL-I 2 of 3).

For UTUC, the POUT trial supports adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy for pT2-4 or N+ disease after nephroureterectomy [344]. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy should be considered for high-risk UTUC as renal function declines after surgery [BL-G 2 of 8].
DPYD testing should be considered before fluoropyrimidine therapy (e.g., capecitabine, 5-FU) to identify patients at risk for severe toxicity [BL-G 6 of 8].

key_trials.full_name: Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy Plus Cystectomy Compared with Cystectomy Alone for Locally Advanced Bladder Cancer
key_trials.intervention: 3 cycles neoadjuvant MVAC followed by radical cystectomy
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 77 months (chemotherapy) vs 46 months (surgery alone); P=0.06; residual disease 15% vs 38% (P<0.001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: 5-year OS not specified; reduction in mortality risk not statistically significant
key_trials.practice_change: Established neoadjuvant chemotherapy as standard for MIBC

key_trials.full_name: Radiotherapy with or without Chemotherapy in Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: 5-year OS 48% vs 35% (P=0.16); no significant increase in grade 3-4 toxicity
key_trials.practice_change: Established chemoradiotherapy as standard for bladder preservation; mitomycin/5-FU preferred radiosensitizer

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab as Second-Line Therapy for Advanced Urothelial Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Advanced urothelial carcinoma progressing after platinum-based chemotherapy
key_trials.comparator: Investigator's choice chemotherapy (paclitaxel, docetaxel, or vinflunine)
key_trials.practice_change: Pembrolizumab became standard second-line therapy (category 1 post-platinum)

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Nivolumab versus Placebo in Muscle-Invasive Urothelial Carcinoma
key_trials.population: High-risk muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma after radical surgery (including UTUC)
key_trials.intervention: Adjuvant nivolumab 240 mg every 2 weeks for up to 1 year
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Disease-free survival (DFS) in intent-to-treat and PD-L1 ≥1% populations
key_trials.practice_change: Adjuvant nivolumab approved for high-risk MIBC/UTUC post-surgery; category 1 for bladder

key_trials.full_name: Avelumab Maintenance Therapy for Advanced or Metastatic Urothelial Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma without progression after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy
key_trials.intervention: Avelumab 10 mg/kg every 2 weeks + best supportive care
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS benefit in all subgroups; grade ≥3 AEs 47.4% vs 25.2%
key_trials.practice_change: Avelumab maintenance became standard (category 1) after first-line platinum chemo

key_trials.full_name: Enfortumab Vedotin and Pembrolizumab in Untreated Advanced Urothelial Cancer
key_trials.population: Previously untreated locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 67.7% vs 44.4%; complete response 29.1% vs 12.5%; grade ≥3 AEs 55.9% vs 69.5%
key_trials.practice_change: Enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab became preferred first-line therapy (category 1) for advanced urothelial carcinoma

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab plus Gemcitabine-Cisplatin in Advanced Urothelial Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Previously untreated unresectable or metastatic urothelial carcinoma
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab + gemcitabine-cisplatin (6 cycles) followed by nivolumab maintenance
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 21.7 vs 18.9 months (HR 0.78; 95% CI 0.63-0.96; P=0.02). PFS at 12 months 34.2% vs 21.8%
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 57.6% vs 43.1%; complete response 21.7% vs 11.8%; grade ≥3 AEs 61.8% vs 51.7%
key_trials.practice_change: Nivolumab + GC is a category 1 first-line option; nivolumab maintenance after chemo

key_trials.full_name: Erdafitinib or Chemotherapy in Advanced or Metastatic Urothelial Carcinoma with FGFR Alterations
key_trials.population: Metastatic urothelial carcinoma with susceptible FGFR3/2 alterations after prior treatment including checkpoint inhibitor
key_trials.practice_change: Erdafitinib is category 1 for FGFR3-mutated advanced urothelial carcinoma post-platinum and post-ICI

key_trials.full_name: Dose-Dense Methotrexate, Vinblastine, Doxorubicin, and Cisplatin or Gemcitabine and Cisplatin as Perioperative Chemotherapy for Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer
key_trials.population: Nonmetastatic muscle-invasive bladder cancer (cT2-T4a N0 M0)
key_trials.intervention: Neoadjuvant or adjuvant ddMVAC (6 cycles) or GC (4-6 cycles)
key_trials.key_result: 3-year PFS 66% (ddMVAC) vs 56% (GC) (HR 0.70; 95% CI 0.51-0.96; P=0.025) in neoadjuvant subgroup. Pathologic complete response 42% vs 36% (P=0.21)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Organ-confined response 77% vs 63% (P=0.001); similar grade ≥3 AEs (52% vs 55%) but different toxicity profiles
key_trials.practice_change: ddMVAC is preferred neoadjuvant regimen over GC for MIBC

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Chemotherapy in Upper Tract Urothelial Carcinoma
key_trials.population: pT2-pT4 or pN1-3 M0 UTUC after nephroureterectomy
key_trials.intervention: Adjuvant gemcitabine + cisplatin or carboplatin (4 cycles)
key_trials.practice_change: Adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy became standard for high-risk UTUC after nephroureterectomy

key_trials.full_name: Primary Chemoablation of Low-Grade Upper Tract Urothelial Carcinoma Using UGN-101 (Mitomycin Reverse Thermal Gel)
key_trials.population: Treatment-naïve or recurrent low-grade noninvasive UTUC (measurable tumor above UPJ)
key_trials.intervention: Mitomycin ureteral gel (6 weekly instillations) with optional monthly maintenance
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Complete response at primary disease evaluation
key_trials.key_result: CR rate 59% (95% CI 47%-71%; P<0.0001). Durability at 12 months 84.2%
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median time to recurrence 13 months; most common AEs: ureteric stenosis, UTI, hematuria
key_trials.practice_change: Approved for low-grade UTUC; alternative to nephroureterectomy in selected patients

key_trials.full_name: TAR-200 (Gemcitabine Intravesical System) for BCG-Unresponsive NMIBC with CIS
key_trials.population: BCG-unresponsive high-risk NMIBC with CIS (with/without Ta/T1)
key_trials.key_result: CR rate 82.4%; median duration of response 25.8 months; 52.9% with DOR ≥12 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade ≥3 AEs 12.9% (urinary tract pain most common); cystectomy-free survival not reported
key_trials.practice_change: TAR-200 approved for BCG-unresponsive NMIBC with CIS; alternative to cystectomy

key_trials.full_name: N-803 (Nogapendekin Alfa Inbakicept-pmln) Plus BCG for BCG-Unresponsive NMIBC
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Complete response rate (Cohort A) and disease-free survival (Cohort B)
key_trials.key_result: Cohort A: CR 71%; median DOR 26.6 months. Cohort B: 12-month DFS 55.4%
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade ≥3 AEs 2% (combination); favorable quality of life
key_trials.practice_change: N-803 + BCG approved for BCG-unresponsive NMIBC with CIS or papillary tumors

key_trials.full_name: ctDNA-Guided Adjuvant Atezolizumab in Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer (Powles et al. 2025)
key_trials.n: Not explicitly stated in source; analysis of IMvigor010 trial
key_trials.population: High-risk MIBC/UTUC post-cystectomy with ctDNA-MRD positivity within 1 year
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Not stated in the guideline; trial is referenced as basis for ctDNA-guided therapy
key_trials.key_result: Atezolizumab improved outcomes in ctDNA-MRD positive patients (category 1 per NCCN)
key_trials.practice_change: ctDNA-MRD testing recommended to determine adjuvant atezolizumab use (category 1) for patients without prior ICI
